title: Listening - Part 4
description: For each question, choose the correct answer.
instructions: You will hear an interview. For questions 20–25, choose the answer (A, B or C) which fits best according to what you hear.
context: You will hear an interview with a man called Sam Carter, who works as a theatre lighting technician.
audio_script: Interviewer: My guest today is Sam Carter, who works as a lighting technician in theatres. Sam, how did you become interested in stage lighting? || Guest: I acted in school plays, but I was never very confident on stage. One day our teacher needed someone to help with the lights. [20]I discovered I preferred creating the atmosphere from the back of the theatre to being watched by the audience[/20]. After that, I volunteered for every performance and learnt by making mistakes. || Interviewer: What do people not realise about your job? || Guest: They think we just press buttons during the show. In reality, [21]most of the work happens before the audience arrives, when we position lights and check every scene carefully[/21]. A small change can make an actor's face disappear into shadow. We have to test everything again and again, even if the show seems simple. || Interviewer: What kind of production do you enjoy most? || Guest: Big musicals are exciting, but I like smaller plays. [22]In a small theatre, a tiny change in colour or brightness can completely alter the mood[/22]. The audience is close to the actors, so lighting has to be gentle and exact. It feels like painting, but with time as well as colour. || Interviewer: Has anything gone wrong during a show? || Guest: Of course. Once a main light failed just before an important scene. [23]I was proud because the team changed the plan quickly and most people in the audience never noticed[/23]. It taught me that you need to stay calm. If you panic, the actors may panic too, and then everyone sees the problem. || Interviewer: Is the job tiring? || Guest: Yes, especially during rehearsals. [24]The hardest part is working late nights and then returning early the next morning to make changes[/24]. I love the work, but sleep becomes difficult. Friends with normal office hours sometimes forget that a theatre day often begins when other people are going home. || Interviewer: What advice would you give someone interested in theatre technology? || Guest: Learn to listen. [25]A good technician understands what the director and actors are trying to create before suggesting equipment[/25]. It's not about showing off with expensive lights. If the story needs one simple lamp, that's what you use. Start by helping local groups; you can learn a lot there. || Interviewer: Do you ever miss acting? || Guest: Occasionally, but only in a friendly way. I understand actors better because I know how exposed they feel under bright lights. My job is to support them, not distract from them. If the audience notices the lighting too much, it may mean I have done something wrong, unless the production deliberately asks for a strong effect. The best compliment is when someone says the show felt magical but cannot explain why. That usually means all the technical work joined together smoothly. I also think patience matters. Some lighting changes take a long time to set up and last only a few seconds on stage, but those seconds can help the audience understand the story.  Good backstage work is quiet, careful, and almost invisible to most people.  I enjoy that responsibility. Always.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: My guest today is Sam Carter, who works as a lighting technician in theatres. Sam, how did you become interested in stage lighting?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: I acted in school plays, but I was never very confident on stage. One day our teacher needed someone to help with the lights. I discovered I preferred creating the atmosphere from the back of the theatre to being watched by the audience. After that, I volunteered for every performance and learnt by making mistakes.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What do people not realise about your job?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: They think we just press buttons during the show. In reality, most of the work happens before the audience arrives, when we position lights and check every scene carefully. A small change can make an actor's face disappear into shadow. We have to test everything again and again, even if the show seems simple.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What kind of production do you enjoy most?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Big musicals are exciting, but I like smaller plays. In a small theatre, a tiny change in colour or brightness can completely alter the mood. The audience is close to the actors, so lighting has to be gentle and exact. It feels like painting, but with time as well as colour.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: Has anything gone wrong during a show?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Of course. Once a main light failed just before an important scene. I was proud because the team changed the plan quickly and most people in the audience never noticed. It taught me that you need to stay calm. If you panic, the actors may panic too, and then everyone sees the problem.

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Yes, especially during rehearsals. The hardest part is working late nights and then returning early the next morning to make changes. I love the work, but sleep becomes difficult. Friends with normal office hours sometimes forget that a theatre day often begins when other people are going home.

dialogue.speaker: interviewer
dialogue.text: What advice would you give someone interested in theatre technology?

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Learn to listen. A good technician understands what the director and actors are trying to create before suggesting equipment. It's not about showing off with expensive lights. If the story needs one simple lamp, that's what you use. Start by helping local groups; you can learn a lot there.

dialogue.speaker: guest
dialogue.text: Occasionally, but only in a friendly way. I understand actors better because I know how exposed they feel under bright lights. My job is to support them, not distract from them. If the audience notices the lighting too much, it may mean I have done something wrong, unless the production deliberately asks for a strong effect. The best compliment is when someone says the show felt magical but cannot explain why. That usually means all the technical work joined together smoothly. I also think patience matters. Some lighting changes take a long time to set up and last only a few seconds on stage, but those seconds can help the audience understand the story.  Good backstage work is quiet, careful, and almost invisible to most people.  I enjoy that responsibility. Always.

questions.question: Why did Sam become interested in lighting?

options.A: He enjoyed working away from the stage.
options.B: He wanted to become a better actor.
options.C: He was asked to design a school theatre.

questions.explanation: Sam says he preferred creating atmosphere from the back of the theatre to being watched.

questions.question: What does Sam say about preparing lights?

options.A: It is easier for simple shows.
options.B: It takes place mostly before the performance.
options.C: It is usually done by the actors.

questions.explanation: He says most of the work happens before the audience arrives.

questions.question: Why does Sam enjoy smaller plays?

options.B: They allow him to work with famous actors.
options.C: Small lighting changes have a big effect.

questions.explanation: Sam says a tiny change in colour or brightness can alter the mood in a small theatre.

questions.question: What happened when a light failed?

options.A: The team solved the problem quickly.
options.B: The audience complained after the show.
options.C: The actors had to stop performing.

questions.explanation: Sam says the team changed the plan quickly and most people did not notice.

questions.question: What does Sam find most difficult?
questions.explanation: He says the hardest part is late nights followed by early mornings.

questions.question: What advice does Sam give future technicians?

options.B: work only with local theatre groups first
options.C: understand the creative aim before choosing lights

questions.explanation: Sam says a good technician understands what the director and actors want before suggesting equipment.
